reviewNotes: The intellectual work, Caxton's print state, the two surviving physical copies, Sommer's three-volume edition, its scan derivatives, the U-M encoding, and the repository artifact are modeled separately.

Sommer volume 1 is the immediate source edition. Volumes 2 and 3 are companion bibliography, apparatus, collation, glossary, and source studies; they are supporting sources, not sources of the encoded readings.

The two Internet Archive objects for Sommer volume 1 are consecutive scan parts: part 1 covers front matter through printed p. 424 and part 2 covers printed pp. 425-861. Neither file alone is a complete scan of the volume.

Sommer's account identifies the Earl Spencer/Althorp copy as his base and the Osterley/Pope copy as the source used to check twenty-one supplied pages. Their documented provenances identify those objects with present Manchester 18930 and Morgan PML 17560.

British Library Additional MS 59678 and Caxton's print preserve materially different versions. The graph treats the manuscript as a supporting parallel witness to a shared earlier textual layer and never as the source from which Sommer or CME was transcribed.

The complete Rylands IIIF bundle is cached at 1200-pixel width per canvas. The encoded textual object maps to canvases 7-867; covers, modern notes, blank and end leaves, and binding views remain in the complete 874-canvas bundle.

The complete surviving Winchester codex is cached as a 970-page public Internet Archive PDF. Its medieval text maps to PDF pp. 11-956 (folios 9r-484v, with the cataloged losses); covers, endleaves, and edge views remain in the file.

Manchester's images are CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. The Winchester Internet Archive item applies a Public Domain Mark, but every manuscript image bears an embedded British Library copyright notice; the conflict is recorded and local caching is not treated as redistribution permission.

The current U-M item says Public Domain / No Copyright-United States, while the 1997 XML header carries restrictive legacy availability terms. Both dated rights layers are preserved pending explicit provider clarification.

The current public raw XML at medictionary.info is not byte-identical to the pinned DLPSTEXTCLASS file. It wraps the text as ETS/EEBO, converts the title page into a DIV1/P, removes EPB milestones, renames HI1/NOTE1, expands correction attributes into 93 CHOICE pairs, and supplies one additional word (‘ye’) at printed p. 249.

lineage.summary: The pinned repository XML and a current public ETS-form XML are two structurally distinct representations of University of Michigan CME item MaloryWks2, a 1997 electronic transcription of the Caxton-derived text in H. Oskar Sommer's 1889 volume 1. Sommer copied the incomplete Althorp/Earl Spencer copy of Caxton's Westminster print of 31 July 1485, now University of Manchester reference 18930, and supplied twenty-one missing printed pages on eleven historical facsimile leaves made from the then-Osterley copy, now Morgan PML 17560; he also had those passages collated against that original. The exact manuscript copy delivered to Caxton is unidentified. British Library Additional MS 59678 is an independent, incomplete parallel witness to the same earlier Malory textual layer, not an upstream source of the CME transcription. Complete local research surrogates preserve all 874 canvases of the Rylands bound print and all 970 PDF pages of the surviving Winchester codex; exact work ranges and image-rights constraints are recorded below.

catalogSummary.author: Sir Thomas Malory (self-named; probably Thomas Malory of Newbold Revel, but exact historical identification remains debated)
catalogSummary.date: Completed between 4 March 1469 and 3 March 1470; first printed 31 July 1485
catalogSummary.editor: William Caxton (1485 recension and twenty-one-book framework); H. Oskar Sommer (1889 source edition)
catalogSummary.title: Le Morte Darthur

catalogingSubject.label: Le Morte Darthur (Caxton recension)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The late-fifteenth-century Middle English Arthurian prose compilation attributed to Sir Thomas Malory as represented by William Caxton's 1485 recension, including Caxton's preface, chapter table, and twenty-one-book/507-chapter organizing framework inherited by Sommer volume 1 and the MaloryWks2 encoding. This cataloging unit is not Sommer's complete three-volume edition, a facsimile of either surviving Caxton copy, or the materially different Winchester version. Caxton's editorial book and chapter divisions are transmission structure, not silently treated as Malory's original authorial divisions.

contentParts.label: Caxton's Preface
contentParts.summary: Caxton defends Arthur's historical reputation, describes receiving a copy of Malory's work, recommends its examples of virtue and avoidance of vice, and announces the twenty-one-book, 507-chapter plan.

contentParts.label: Caxton's Table or Rubric of Chapters
notes: The table announces the same 507 chapters counted under the twenty-one book parts, so its rows are not counted a second time in the intellectual extent.
contentParts.summary: A book-by-book table summarizes the announced chapters of the twenty-one-book recension and exposes several discrepancies with the printed chapter sequence that the electronic editors document rather than erase.

contentParts.label: Book I: Arthur's Birth and Early Reign
notes: Caxton announces 28 chapters; the pinned XML has 25 DIV2 containers because chapter organization and headings do not map one-to-one.
contentParts.summary: Uther Pendragon fathers Arthur through Merlin's device; Arthur is raised in concealment, proves his kingship through the sword in the stone, is crowned, fights the rebel kings, receives Excalibur, and sets in motion the tragedy of Mordred through the May Day children.

contentParts.label: Book II: The Tale of Balin and Balan
contentParts.summary: Balin draws the fatal sword, helps overthrow King Rience, strikes the Dolorous Stroke at King Pellam's castle, and unknowingly kills and is killed by his brother Balan.

contentParts.label: Book III: Arthur's Marriage and the Round Table
contentParts.summary: Arthur marries Guinevere, receives the Round Table, and sends Gawain, Tor, and Pellinore on quests whose judgments establish both knightly fellowship and its moral hazards.

contentParts.label: Book IV: Merlin, Morgan le Fay, and Knightly Adventures
notes: Caxton announces 29 chapters; the pinned XML has 28 DIV2 containers.
contentParts.summary: Merlin is imprisoned through his desire for Nimue; Arthur defeats five kings and survives Morgan le Fay's plot through Accolon; Gawain, Ywain, Marhaus, and Pelleas pursue interwoven adventures.

contentParts.label: Book V: Arthur's Roman War
contentParts.summary: Arthur rejects Roman tribute, kills the giant of Mont Saint-Michel, defeats Emperor Lucius, campaigns through Europe, and is crowned emperor.

contentParts.label: Book VI: Sir Lancelot du Lake
contentParts.summary: Lancelot and Lionel seek adventures; Lancelot escapes four enchantress queens, defeats Sir Turquyn, frees prisoners, enters the Chapel Perilous, and returns to court with his reputation enlarged.

contentParts.label: Book VII: The Tale of Sir Gareth of Orkney
notes: Caxton announces 36 chapters; the pinned XML has 35 DIV2 containers.
contentParts.summary: Gareth arrives anonymously as Beaumains, endures Lynet's contempt, defeats the colored knights and the Red Knight of the Red Lands, reveals his identity, wins Lyoness, and is reconciled with his kin.

contentParts.label: Book VIII: Sir Tristram de Lyones
contentParts.summary: Tristram's birth, education, combat with Marhaus, healing by Isode, love potion, service to King Mark, early rivalry with Palomides, and widening fellowship with Arthurian knights begin the long Tristram sequence.

contentParts.label: Book IX: La Cote Male Taile and Tristram
contentParts.summary: The adventures of La Cote Male Taile and Maledisant lead back into Tristram's conflicts with Palomides and King Mark, his exile and madness, and his encounters with Arthur and Lancelot.

contentParts.label: Book X: Tristram and Other Adventures
contentParts.summary: The largest Caxton book interlaces Tristram and Isode, Palomides, Lamorak, Dinadan, Lancelot, King Mark, tournaments, factional violence, and Joyous Gard, ending with the deferred combat between Tristram and Palomides.

contentParts.label: Book XI: Lancelot and Galahad
contentParts.summary: At Corbenic Lancelot is deceived into begetting Galahad on Elaine; Bors witnesses the child and the Grail, Guinevere's jealousy drives Lancelot into madness, and Percival and Ector encounter Grail miracles.

contentParts.label: Book XII: Lancelot's Madness and Recovery
contentParts.summary: The mad Lancelot wanders, fights, and suffers before Elaine recognizes him and the Grail heals him; he lives at Joyous Isle, is restored to his companions, and the Tristram sequence closes with Palomides' baptism.

contentParts.label: Book XIII: The Quest of the Holy Grail Begins
contentParts.summary: Galahad is knighted, occupies the Siege Perilous, draws the sword from the stone, receives the white shield, and joins the Round Table's vow to seek the Grail; Lancelot begins penitential recognition of his failures.

contentParts.label: Book XIV: Percival's Grail Trials
contentParts.summary: Percival receives instruction on the Grail knights, survives supernatural dangers and allegorical visions, resists demonic temptation, and undertakes bodily penance.

contentParts.label: Book XV: Lancelot's Grail Trials
contentParts.summary: Lancelot encounters holy signs and visions, learns from hermits that Galahad is his son, and confronts the spiritual consequences of his worldly attachments.

contentParts.label: Book XVI: Gawain, Ector, Bors, and Lionel
contentParts.summary: Gawain and Ector's visions expose their failures, while Bors accepts penance, rescues a maiden instead of his brother, resists temptation, and survives Lionel's murderous rage.

contentParts.label: Book XVII: The Achievement of the Holy Grail
contentParts.summary: Galahad, Percival, Bors, and Percival's sister travel by the miraculous ship; her sacrificial death enables a healing; the Grail knights reach Corbenic, heal the Maimed King, and achieve the Grail before Galahad and Percival die.

contentParts.label: Book XVIII: Lancelot, Guinevere, and Elaine of Astolat
contentParts.summary: After the Grail quest Lancelot returns to Guinevere; he clears her of a poisoning charge, fights at the Winchester tournament wearing Elaine of Astolat's sleeve, and receives the dead maiden's letter after her unreturned love destroys her.

contentParts.label: Book XIX: Guinevere's Abduction and the Healing of Urry
contentParts.summary: Meliagaunt abducts Guinevere; Lancelot rides in the cart to rescue her, secretly visits her chamber, defeats her accuser, and later miraculously heals Sir Urry after the other Round Table knights fail.

contentParts.label: Book XX: The Most Piteous Tale of the Morte Arthur
contentParts.summary: Agravain and Mordred expose Lancelot and Guinevere; Lancelot's rescue of the queen kills fellow knights and splits the Round Table, Arthur wars against Lancelot, and the dying Gawain seeks reconciliation as Mordred usurps the realm.

contentParts.label: Book XXI: Arthur's Last Departure and the End of the Fellowship
notes: Chapter 7 introduces ‘Hic iacet Arthurus Rex quondam Rex que futurus’ as a ‘vers’. It is embedded inline in prose, and no semantic verse-line divisions are encoded.
contentParts.summary: Mordred's rule, the final battle, Arthur's departure with the queens, Guinevere's convent life, and Lancelot's penitence and death close the fellowship; chapter 7 embeds Arthur's Latin epitaph, and Malory's prayer and Caxton's dated colophon end the book.

extent.description: The intellectual extent is 507 announced chapters under twenty-one book sections. Book XXI chapter 7 embeds one Latin epitaph that Caxton calls a ‘vers’, so that chapter is counted as mixed; semantic verse-line count is unknown because neither the XML nor Caxton's print encodes lineation. The pinned XML has 23 top-level DIV1 units (preface, table, and 21 books) but only 502 actual DIV2 chapter containers: Book I has 25 containers for 28 announced chapters, Book IV has 28 for 29, and Book VII has 35 for 36 because of combined, missing, or unlisted chapter structures. The other eighteen books' container counts equal their announced counts. The 502 encoding containers are therefore preserved as representation data rather than substituted for the 507-chapter intellectual extent.

subjects.label: Death, repentance, and the fall of the Arthurian fellowship

summaries.value: Malory's Middle English prose compilation gathers and reshapes French and English Arthurian narratives into the rise, flourishing, spiritual testing, fracture, and fall of King Arthur's fellowship. Its interlaced stories include Arthur's birth and establishment of the realm, the careers of Lancelot, Gareth, Tristram, Galahad, Percival, Bors, and many other knights, the quest for the Holy Grail, the love of Lancelot and Guinevere, Mordred's rebellion, Arthur's last battle and departure, and the penitential deaths of the survivors.

The encoded Caxton recension contains Caxton's preface and chapter table followed by twenty-one books: early Arthur and Merlin (Books I-IV), the Roman campaign (V), Lancelot and Gareth (VI-VII), the long Tristram complex (VIII-X), Galahad's conception and Lancelot's madness (XI-XII), the Grail quest (XIII-XVII), Lancelot and Guinevere (XVIII-XIX), and the destruction and aftermath of Arthur's realm (XX-XXI).

This record follows the Caxton recension's preface/table/twenty-one-book boundary because that is the exact intellectual presentation encoded by MaloryWks2. It does not project Caxton's divisions backward as Malory's undisputed original structure, and it treats the Winchester manuscript as a related, independent version rather than an upstream source.

assertion.notes: Caxton's 1485 colophon applies this title to the whole printed book.

notes: The ending calls the work the whole book of King Arthur and his noble knights; the scholarly project distinguishes this Malory-associated wording from Caxton's conventional whole-book title.
